coredata.dc:title: Preparation of porous organic polymer nanoparticles in miniemulsion for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Qihao
Guo, Xue
Zhang, Wei
Dong, Wenyue
Duan, Qian
coredata.dc:description: Nitroaromatic explosives, especially 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), are not only endanger national security as high explosives, but also cause harm to environmental protection and human health by the pollution of the soil and groundwater from related industrial production, thus it is of great significance to achieve a highly sensitive and selective TNP detection, especially in pure water media. Here, two novel porous organic polymer nanoparticles PTATE2 and PTATE4 based on tetraphenylethene groups and electron-rich triphenylamine units were prepared successfully by Suzuki coupling (A2+B3, A4+B3) in miniemulsion, and fully characterized by FT-IR, NMR, SEM, nitrogen sorption isotherm, CV curves, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. PTATE2 and PTATE4 possess spherical particle morphology with sizes of 90 nm and 64 nm, and porous structure with pore diameters of 4.3 and 4.8 nm, respectively, demonstrating that porous PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles were obtained. The fluorescent sensing properties of PTATE2 and PTATE4 towards nitroaromatic explosives in aqueous media show a sensitive and selective detection towards TNP, with the Stern-Volmer quenching constants and detection limits of 1.8 × 104 M−1 and 722 nM for PTATE2, 2.4 × 104 M−1 and 564 nM for PTATE4, respectively. Moreover, the spike/recovery tests in actual water samples indicates the recovery rates of TNP are 94.76%–103.82% by PTATE2 and 93.35%–100.72% by PTATE4, and the t-tests show that there is no significant difference between the determination results of present fluorescence sensing method based on PTATE2 and PTATE4 and HPLC analysis at 95% confidence level. The study of sensing mechanism demonstrates that the inner filter effect is dominant in the process of fluorescence quenching towards TNP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous organic polymer
Nitroaromatic explosives
Fluorescence sensing
Nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of PTATE2 (black) and PTATE4 (red).
SEM images of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (c), and particle size distributions of PTATE2 (b) and PTATE4 (d).
The nitrogen isothermal adsorption and desorption curves of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (c), and pore size distributions of PTATE2 (b) and PTATE4 (d).

UV–Vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (c), and CIE chromaticity diagrams of PTATE2 (b) and PTATE4 (d) in water. (λex for PTATE2 = 383 nm and λex for PTATE4 = 388 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (c) aqueous dispersions with TNP addition. Stern-Volmer plots of relative fluorescence intensities (I0/I-1) of PTATE2 (b) and PTATE4 (d) vs. TNP concentration. The insets of Fig. 5(a, c) show fluorescent images of PTATE2 and PTAPE4 before and after TNP addition. (λex for PTATE2 = 383 nm and λex for PTATE4 = 388 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (c) dispersions with TNB addition. Stern-Volmer plots of relative fluorescence intensities (I0/I–1) of PTATE2 (b) and PTATE4 (d) vs. TNB concentration. (λex for PTATE2 = 383 nm and λex for PTATE4 = 388 nm).

Fluorescence spectra of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (c) solutions with DNT addition. Stern-Volmer plots of relative fluorescence intensities (I0/I-1) of PTATE2 (b) and PTATE4 (d) vs. DNT concentration. (λex for PTATE2 = 383 nm and λex for PTATE4 = 388 nm).
Competitive detection of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (b) towards TNP and other nitro compounds (phenol as comparison). (λex for PTATE2 = 383 nm and λex for PTATE4 = 388 nm).
Cyclic voltammetry curves of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (b) in acetonitrile (electrolyte: 0.1 M TBAPF6).
Schematic diagram of HOMO and LUMO energy levels of PTATE2, PTATE4 and nitroaromatic explosives.
UV–Vis spectra of TNP, TNB and DNT, and excitation and fluorescence spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4. (λex for PTATE2 = 383 nm and λex for PTATE4 = 388 nm).
The time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of PTATE2 (a) and PTATE4 (b).
The synthetic routes of PTATE2 and PTATE4.
Statistical data of spiked/recovery tests in actual water samples for TNP determination.

head.ce:title: Preparation of porous organic polymer nanoparticles in miniemulsion for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, PR China

Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitroaromatic explosives, especially 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), are not only endanger national security as high explosives, but also cause harm to environmental protection and human health by the pollution of the soil and groundwater from related industrial production, thus it is of great significance to achieve a highly sensitive and selective TNP detection, especially in pure water media. Here, two novel porous organic polymer nanoparticles PTATE2 and PTATE4 based on tetraphenylethene groups and electron-rich triphenylamine units were prepared successfully by Suzuki coupling (A2+B3, A4+B3) in miniemulsion, and fully characterized by FT-IR, NMR, SEM, nitrogen sorption isotherm, CV curves, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. PTATE2 and PTATE4 possess spherical particle morphology with sizes of 90 nm and 64 nm, and porous structure with pore diameters of 4.3 and 4.8 nm, respectively, demonstrating that porous PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles were obtained. The fluorescent sensing properties of PTATE2 and PTATE4 towards nitroaromatic explosives in aqueous media show a sensitive and selective detection towards TNP, with the Stern-Volmer quenching constants and detection limits of 1.8 × 104 M−1 and 722 nM for PTATE2, 2.4 × 104 M−1 and 564 nM for PTATE4, respectively. Moreover, the spike/recovery tests in actual water samples indicates the recovery rates of TNP are 94.76%–103.82% by PTATE2 and 93.35%–100.72% by PTATE4, and the t-tests show that there is no significant difference between the determination results of present fluorescence sensing method based on PTATE2 and PTATE4 and HPLC analysis at 95% confidence level. The study of sensing mechanism demonstrates that the inner filter effect is dominant in the process of fluorescence quenching towards TNP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The existence of explosives is closely related to national security. Once used by terrorists, social peace and people's property will be greatly threatened [1–3]. Among all kinds of nitroaromatic explosives, 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP, also called picric acid) are widely used due to their greatly powerful explosive properties [4–6], and their toxic degradation products had caused severe pollution to soil and groundwater at war zones [7]. Meanwhile, the widely used nitroaromatics in the fields of dyes, medicine, agriculture, etc., could also cause pollution to our environment. Therefore, it is urgent to develop high-performance explosive detection methods with high sensitivity, high selectivity, low cost and portability. After years of efforts, many methods have been proposed to detect explosives, such as ion mobility spectrometry [8], energy dispersive X-ray diffraction [9], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [10], gas chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry [11] and so on [12]. Since the above-mentioned approaches demand bulky costly equipment, or complicated manipulation, or inconvenience to carry [13], the researchers began to pay attention to the fluorescence sensing method, owing to their remarkable sensitivity, high-speed, convenience and low cost [14–16].

Many materials have been developed to apply in fluorescence-based sensor, such as covalent organic frameworks [17,18], metal organic frameworks [19,20], conjugated polymers [21,22], quantum dots [23–26], porous organic polymers (POPs) [27,28] and so on [29–31]. POPs possess several merits, such as high specific surface area, permanent porosity [32], chemical stability [33], structural flexibility [34] and low density [35]. However, the traditional POPs are insoluble or not well-dispersible in water due to their highly cross-linked structure, which severely limits their application in aqueous phase. In this paper, two novel POP nanoparticles PTATE2 and PTATE4 based on tetraphenylethene (TPE) groups and triphenylamine (TPA) units were designed and prepared successfully by Suzuki coupling in miniemulsion. The prepared two POP nanoparticles PTATE2 and PTATE4 with small particle sizes and porous structures could be well dispersed in aqueous phase. TPA derivatives have excellent electron donating property, which is conducive to the interaction with electron deficient nitroaromatic explosives [36,37]. According to the fluorescence titration experiments, PTATE2 and PTATE4 achieved high sensitivity and selectivity for TNP detection, with the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (KSV) values one order of magnitude higher than the other nitroaromatics. In addition, the spike/recovery tests of TNP in actual water samples were performed and t-tests prove the accuracy of our fluorescence sensing method compared with HPLC at 95% confidence level. At last, the sensing mechanism for the sensitivity and selectivity of TNP detection based on PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles was discussed in detail by cyclic voltammetry, UV–Vis absorption, excitation and fluorescence spectra, fluorescence decay curves, etc.
The details of reagents and measurements are given in Supplementary Information (SI).

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the synthesis procedure of PTATE2 nanoparticles is as follows. Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) (0.20 g, 0.69 mmol) and K2CO3 (0.25 g, 1.80 mmol) were dissolved in deionized water (20 mL), then ultrasonically treated for 15 min. TPA triboronic ester (0.16 g, 0.20 mmol), TPE-2Br (0.15 g, 0.30 mmol), tetra(triphenylphosphine)palladium (Pd(PPh3)4) (0.01 g, 0.01 mmol) were dissolved in toluene (2 mL), and ultrasonically treated for 15 min. The two solutions were mixed and ultrasonicated for 10 min to form a stable miniemulsion. The miniemulsion was degassed by freeze-pump-thaw for three times and heated at 85 °C for 24 h. The obtained mixture was stirred in the air to evaporate toluene and then loaded in dialyzer membrane (Mn 3500 g/mol) and placed in water for dialysis. After dialysis, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to obtain polymer PTATE2 nanoparticles. Yield: 46%.
As illustrated in Scheme 1, the preparation of PTATE4 nanoparticles was similar to that of PTATE2 nanoparticles with different TPE-4Br amount of (0.10 g, 0.15 mmol). Yield: 50%.

Scheme 1 describes the synthetic routes of PTATE2 and PTATE4 by crosslinking polymerization in miniemulsion. The TPA triboronic ester was synthesized according to the literature [38]. The synthetic procedures and structural characterization of dibromo-substituted tetraphenylethene (TPE-2Br) and tetrabromo-substituted tetraphenylethene (TPE-4Br) are given in SI (Scheme S1, Figs. S1–S4). The crosslinked polymers PTATE2 and PTATE4 were synthesized using monomers TPE-2Br or TPE-4Br and TPA triboronic ester via typical Suzuki coupling in toluene & water o/w miniemulsion, with Pd(PPh3)4 as the catalyst, K2CO3 as base and SDS as surfactant. The miniemulsion polymerization is a convenient method to prepare organic polymer nanoparticles. The chemical structures of the obtained PTATE2 and PTATE4 were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 1 ), 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra (Fig. S5) and 13C NMR spectra (Figs. S6–S7). In Fig. 1, the FT-IR spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4 exhibit nearly identical peaks due to their similar structures. The peaks near 2926 and 2853 cm−1 are C–H stretching of the phenyl rings. The peaks at 1144 and 700 cm−1 are attributed to the C–H bending vibration of the phenyl rings, while the peaks at 1597 and 1492 cm−1 come from the CC stretching of the phenyl rings. The C–N bending vibration of aromatic tertiary amine appears at 1359 cm−1. The formation of polymers PTATE2 and PTATE4 were further corroborated by 1H NMR spectra, as shown in Fig. S5. The TPE-2Br, TPE-4Br and TPA triboronic ester monomers show clear peaks with exact assignments of hydrogen atoms from the 1H NMR spectra. Compared with the monomers, 1H NMR spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4 are more complicated due to their highly crosslinking structure. The widening peak and shifting peak position of the polymers indicate the successful synthesis of PTATE2 and PTATE4.

The morphologies of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The obtained polymer dispersions were deposited on silicon plates for SEM, and the SEM images are shown in Fig. 2 . Obviously, PTATE2 and PTATE4 possess spherical particle morphologies with uniform particle sizes from the SEM images (Fig. 2 (a) and (c)). The average particle sizes of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles are ca. 90 nm and ca. 64 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 (b) and (d). Furthermore, dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were carried out to confirm the nano-sized particles of the obtained polymers. As shown in Fig. S9, the average hydrodynamic diameters of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were measured to be ca. 83 nm and 75 nm, respectively. The well-defined nano-sizes of the polymers ensure their good dispersion in water, which provides the possibility for detecting nitroaromatic explosives in the pure aqueous phase.

Nitrogen isothermal adsorption and desorption experiments of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were carried out to measure their surface area, pore volume and pore size. As shown in Fig. 3 , hysteresis loops appear when P/P0 is 0–1. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of PTATE2 is 20.3 m2/g, the pore diameter is mainly distributed at 4.3 nm, and the pore volume is 0.06 cm3/g. As for PTATE4, the BET surface area is 13.6 m2/g, the pore size was determined to be 4.8 nm, and the pore volume is 0.14 cm3/g. These data indicate that PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles possess porous structures, which is favorable for fluorescence sensing. Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurement of the as-synthesized PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles was further performed, as shown in Fig. S8. Both of the PXRD patterns show broad peaks at ca. 20°, indicating the absence of a long range order in the structures of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles.

The photophysical properties of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles in water were recorded by UV–Vis absorption, excitation and fluorescence spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (c). The wide absorption bands at around 313 nm for PTATE2 and 310 nm for PTATE4 correspond to the π-π* transition of the polymeric backbones. The maximum excitation wavelengths of PTATE2 and PTATE4 are 383 nm and 388 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectrum of PTATE2 centers at 487 nm shows a blue emission (Fig. 4 (b)), while PTATE4 centers at 495 nm indicating a bluish-green light emission (Fig. 4 (d)). Compared with PTATE2, the emission peak of PTATE4 shows a redshift of 8 nm, which could be attributed to the enhanced conjugative interaction in PTATE4.

As mentioned above, POP nanoparticles PTATE2 and PTATE4 could be used as fluorescence sensing materials for nitroaromatics. Firstly, the electron-rich TPA structure in the polymeric backbone is beneficial for the interaction with electron-deficient nitroaromatics. Secondly, the porosity and large specific surface area of the polymers are conducive to the adsorption/diffusion of nitroaromatic analytes. In addition, the obtained polymer nanoparticles could be well-dispersed in pure water. Thus the fluorescence sensing properties of the polymers towards nitroaromatic explosives in aqueous media were investigated.

Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence spectra and Stern-Volmer plots of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles in the pure aqueous dispersion with different amounts of TNP. With the increasing TNP concentration, the fluorescence intensities of the two polymers gradually decrease, indicating their quenching capability to TNP. Notably, the shape and peak position of the fluorescence spectra did not change significantly after the addition of TNP, which proves no new fluorescence substances formed during the quenching process. For PTATE2 nanoparticles, the fluorescence was obviously quenched with a quenching degree of 87% with the TNP concentration increasing from 0 to 222 μM (Fig. 5 (a)). The quenching constant KSV value of PTATE2 is 1.8 × 104 M−1 in the TNP concentration lower than 63 μM, indicating a high sensitivity to detect TNP (Fig. 5 (b)). As shown in Fig. 5 (c) and (d), in the fluorescence sensing experiment based on PTATE4, the fluorescence quenching degree of PTATE4 reached 89% after 140 μM of TNP was added and the KSV value was determined to be 2.4 × 104 M−1. Afterwards, the limits of detection (LODs) and the limits of quantification (LOQs) were calculated according to LOD = 3 σ/K and LOQ = 10 σ/K [39], where σ is the standard deviation of blank signal, K represents the slope of the linear fitting curve between TNP concentration and fluorescence intensity of polymer (Fig. S10). The LOD values of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were calculated as 722 nM and 564 nM, respectively, while LOQ values are 2.40 μM and 1.90 μM, respectively (Tables S1–S2). The high KSV, low LOD and LOQ values indicate that PTATE2 and PTATE4 have a high sensitivity for TNP detection in aqueous media.

Selective detection is crucial for the practical application of explosive fluorescence sensing. As shown in Figs. 6 and 7
, the fluorescence sensing properties of PTATE2 and PTATE4 towards 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene (TNB) and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) in water were tested. With the added concentration of TNB and DNT up to 1.3 mM and 1.5 mM, the fluorescence of PTATE2 and PTATE4 dispersions decrease gradually, with the quenching degrees of 66% and 58%, respectively. The KSV, LOD and LOQ values for TNB detection were determined to be 1.5 × 103 M−1, 9.38 μM and 31.3 μM for PTATE2, 2.5 × 103 M−1, 18.8 μM and 62.7 μM for PTATE4, respectively (Fig. S11, Tables S1–S2). As for DNT detection, the KSV, LOD and LOQ values were determined to be 1.1 × 103 M−1, 12.0 μM and 40.1 μM for PTATE2, 8.3 × 102 M−1, 4.89 μM and 16.3 μM for PTATE4, respectively (Fig. S11, Tables S1–S2). The polymers exhibit higher sensitivity for TNB detection than DNT, possibly because there are one more electron-deficient nitro groups in TNB, causing a stronger interaction with PTATE2 and PTATE4. Compared with TNP detection, the LOD and LOQ values towards TNB and DNT are much higher, and the KSV values are one order of magnitude lower, demonstrating the high sensitivity and selectivity of PTATE2 and PTATE4 towards TNP detection in aqueous media.

The competitive characteristics of PTATE2 and PTATE4 for TNP detection in aqueous phase were investigated. As shown in Fig. 8 , upon individually adding DNT, TNB, nitrobenzene (NB), 4-nitrobenzoic acid (NBA), phenol, imidacloprid (ICP), 2,3-dimethyl-2,3-dinitrobutane (DMDNB) and nitrocyclohexane (NCH) into the aqueous polymer dispersion, the same amount of TNP was added. As expected, the fluorescence intensities of PTATE2 and PTATE4 show no obvious change after the addition of DNT, TNB, NB, NBA, phenol, ICP, DMDNB and NCH, respectively, due to their weak quenching effects. While after adding the same amount of TNP, the fluorescence intensities of both polymers significantly decreased, which are comparable to the data of adding TNP alone, thus demonstrating PTATE2 and PTATE4 have a high selectivity and anti-interference characteristic for TNP detection in the presence of other nitro compounds.

Based on the high sensitivity and selectivity of PTATE2 and PTATE4 for TNP detection in aqueous phase, the spectrofluorimetric TNP detection in actual water samples was carried out by standard spike/recovery method. The water samples including deionized water, mineral water and tap water were added scalars of 5.00 mg/L and 10.00 mg/L, respectively. According to the relative fluorescence intensities of the polymers vs the corresponding TNP concentration (mg/L) in Fig. S12 (a) and (b), linear regression equations of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were determined as y = 0.07786 [TNP] - 0.03345 (R2 = 0.9964) and y = 0.09934 [TNP] - 0.05173 (R2 = 0.9894), respectively. The statistical data of spiked/recovery tests in actual water samples for TNP determination are shown in Table 1 . The recovery rates of TNP were found to be 94.76%–103.82% measured by PTATE2, and 93.35%–100.72% measured by PTATE4, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) of PTATE2 and PTATE4 for TNP content analysis in various water samples are in the range of 1.59%–6.96% and 1.24%–3.08%, respectively. To test the reliability of our sensing method, HPLC method was adopted to analyze the content of TNP in different water samples. The calibration curve of HPLC analysis is shown in Fig. S12 (c). As shown in Table 1, the obtained HPLC analysis data are basically consistent with the fluorescence spectrophotometry using PTATE2 and PTATE4 as sensing materials. In addition, the t-test method was adopted to evaluate the obtained data, and the statistical analyses are shown in Tables S3–S5. The t-test data show that there is no significant difference between the determination results of our polymer fluorescence sensing method and HPLC analysis at 95% confidence level (texp. < 4.30). The above results demonstrate that PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles could be applied in the highly sensitive, selective and reliable detection of trace TNP in aqueous media.

In order to further understand the experimental phenomena, the sensing mechanism was explored. The electrochemical properties of polymers PTATE2 and PTATE4 were studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV). As shown in Fig. 9 , PTATE2 and PTATE4 show obvious oxidation processes due to the electron-rich polyTPA backbones, with initial oxidation potentials of 0.81 V and 0.89 V, respectively. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were calculated to be −5.17 eV and −5.25 eV, respectively, according to the equation HOMO = - (Eonset OX + 4.36) eV [40]. The optical band gaps of PTATE2 and PTATE4 were determined to be 2.87 eV and 2.86 eV, respectively, according to the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the polymers, and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were calculated to be −2.30 eV and −2.39 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10 , the LUMO energy levels of PTATE2 and PTATE4 are higher than that of TNP, TNB and DNT, thus the photo-induced electron transfer mechanism may exist in the fluorescence quenching process [41,42]. Moreover, the LUMO energy level of TNP is lower than that of TNB and DNT, which may be one of the reasons for the high sensitivity of the polymers towards TNP.

The excitation and emission spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4 and the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TNP, TNB and DNT were tested. As shown in Fig. 11 , only the absorption spectrum of TNP overlaps with the emission spectra of the two polymers, so Fӧster resonance energy transfer may exist in the fluorescence quenching process of TNP. Meanwhile, the absorption spectrum of TNP overlaps with the excitation spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4 to a great extent, therefore, fluorescence inner filter effect in TNP sensing may also exist.

To further confirm the sensing mechanism, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4 aqueous dispersion in the absence and presence of TNP were recorded, as shown in Fig. S13. There is no extra absorbance band can be observed from the UV–Vis absorption spectra, which is consistent with inner filter effect mechanism [43,44]. Fig. 12 shows the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of PTATE2 and PTATE4, and there is almost no change in the fluorescence lifetimes of PTATE2 and PTATE4 after the addition of TNP. Furthermore, the time resolved PL-based titration experiments of PTATE2 and PTATE4 aqueous dispersion with different TNP concentrations (Fig. S14) were measured, and τ0/τ of PTATE2 and PTATE4 aqueous dispersion vs TNP concentration were linearly fitted and parallel to the x axis, showing almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes upon TNP addition, thus demonstrating that the fluorescence inner filter effect is the primary mechanism for the better sensitivity and selectivity of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles towards TNP detection.

Two novel porous organic polymer nanoparticles PTATE2 and PTATE4 based on TPE groups and electron-rich TPA units with good dispersibility in water have been successfully prepared by Suzuki-miniemulsion polymerization. The fluorescence sensing properties of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles towards nitroaromatics were investigated in pure water, and highly sensitive and selective detection towards TNP was obtained, with KSV values of 1.8 × 104 M−1 and 2.4 × 104 M−1, LODs of 722 nM and 564 nM, LOQs of 2.40 μM and 1.90 μM, respectively. In addition, the polymer nanoparticles were utilized for detecting TNP in actual water samples by spike/recovery method, and the results show no significant difference between the fluorescence sensing method of PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles and HPLC analysis under 95% confidence (t-test). The porous organic polymer PTATE2 and PTATE4 nanoparticles exhibit a great prospect in the fluorescence detection of TNP in pure aqueous phase.

Qihao Cui: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xue Guo: Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Wei Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Wenyue Dong: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Qian Duan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support from Science and Technology Development Plan Project of Jilin Province [20200201235JC]; Science and Technology Research Project of the Education Department of Jilin Province [JJKH20210826KJ]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [51803013].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed terbium-doped blue carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen B B, Liu Z X, Zou H Y, Huang C Z. Highly selective detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by using newly developed terbium-doped blue carbon dots. Analyst 2016;141:2676-2681.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly and sensitive fluorescent paper sensor for nitroaromatic explosive detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ma Y, Li H, Peng S, Wang L. Highly and sensitive fluorescent paper sensor for nitroaromatic explosive detection. Anal. Chem. 2012;84(19):8415-8421.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescein based fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for sensitive detection of TNP explosive in aqueous medium: application of logic gate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ilyas Q, Waseem M T, Junaid H M, Khan Z A, Munir F, Shaikh A J, Shahzad S A. Fluorescein based fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for sensitive detection of TNP explosive in aqueous medium: application of logic gate. Spectrochim. Acta. A 2022;272:1386-1425.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: AIE or AIE(P)E-active transition metal complexes for highly sensitive detection of nitroaromatic explosives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ramdass A, Sathish V, Thanasekaran P. AIE or AIE(P)E-active transition metal complexes for highly sensitive detection of nitroaromatic explosives. Results in Chemistry 2022;4:2211-7156,

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step synthesis of novel photoluminescent nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots from allylamine for highly sensitive and selective fluorescence detection of trinitrophenol and fluorescent ink

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ran X, Qu Q, Lei L, Zuo L, Zhang S, Gui J, Kang Y, Long Y. One-step synthesis of novel photoluminescent nitrogen-rich carbon nanodots from allylamine for highly sensitive and selective fluorescence detection of trinitrophenol and fluorescent ink. ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 2018;6:11716-11723.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of vapor phase trinitrotoluene in the parts-per-trillion range using waveguide interferometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Edmiston P L, Campbell D P, Gottfried D S, Baughman J, Timmers M M. Detection of vapor phase trinitrotoluene in the parts-per-trillion range using waveguide interferometry. Sensor Actuat. B-Chem 2010;143:574-582.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent detection of 2,4-DNT and 2,4,6-TNT in aqueous media by using simple water-soluble pyrene derivatives

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kovalev I S, Taniya O S, Slovesnova N V, Kim G A, Santra S, Zyryanov G V, Kopchuk D S, Majee A, Charushin V N, Chupakhin O N.
